content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What’s a movie that genuinely made you laugh out loud?

**Score:** 82
**Body:** Hey folks, I’ve been feeling kind of low lately and could really use a solid laugh, the kind that makes your face hurt or catches you off guard with how funny it is.

Any genre, any era,  I’m open to anything. I just want to forget about life for a bit and have a good time.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Walk Hard  The Dewey Cox Story
2. Tucker and Dale vs Evil

Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves (Mainly the Jarnathan scenes)

Step Brothers
3. Tommy Boy
4. Team America: World Police
5. Superbad (2007)
6. Wet hot American summer 
7. What about Bob?
8. I will smirk or chuckle at movies..... But even Airplane/naked gun type movies i find just amusing.

The movie that made me LOL?

"A fish called wanda"

I'm originally from the UK, so the humor landed well with me
9. Hot Fuzz. My personal gold standard for hard, smart laughs.
10. Fletch. Best In Show. Waiting

## 2. The Conversation (1974) is a mystery thriller masterpiece about surveillance and paranoia that's terrifyingly relevant

**Score:** 68
**Body:** One of Francis Ford Coppola's best movies, it stars Gene Hackman as a surveillance expert Harry Caul who becomes obsessed with a recording he makes in a San Francisco park. While piecing together the seemingly innocuous dialogue between a young couple, Harry discovers a hidden message suggesting a potential murder. 

In addition to Hackman, the film stars John Cazale, and an early role for Harrison Ford. 


This movie was prescient in showing the potential for technology to erode personal privacy. It won the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival. 

Roger Ebert's described Hackman's portrayal as "one of the most affecting and tragic characters in the movies", a "career peak". Fun fact: Hackman's performance in the lead role was listed the 37th greatest in movie history by Premiere magazine in 2006.

The film is absolutely brilliant.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s a great role for Hackman.

May I recommend, if you haven’t seen it already, the movie *Night Moves* (1975).  It’s a wonderful “modern” take on the pulp detective genre, mixing up Raymond Chandler’s *The Big Sleep* with a bit of Dashiell Hammett’s *The Maltese Falcon* yet charting its own path as well.

I feel the roles Hackman plays in *The Conversation* and *Night Moves* are fascinating both in similar and opposite ways.  In *The Conversation* Hackman’s character is undoubtedly a very smart man who ultimately succumbs and becomes a victim to the very thing he is supposed to know… surveillance.  In *Night Moves* Hackman plays a P.I. who is, unlike the cliched usual P.I.s who seem to know almost supernaturally what’s going on and what to do in the case, constantly a step or two *behind* everything, missing clues and stumbling toward a tragic end because he’s just not a very good P.I.

Now, before you think this is an Inspector Clouseau type of situation, we feel total empathy for Hackman’s P.I.  We see he’s trying his best in the situation he’s in but he just cannot succeed because he doesn’t have the intellect for what he’s facing.  This is spelled out beautifully in the movie’s opening minutes when his character discovers, quite by accident, that his wife is cheating on him.

Think about it: You’re a P.I. who’s supposed to be clever and see through people and situations and lies… and you didn’t even know your own wife was cheating on you?!  And so it goes… wonderfully!
2. Hackman plays a similar role in Enemy of the State (1998)
  - Same character in all but name and age, really. Kind of a “Carter from ER” vs. The Pitt situation.
3. I love this movie. It’s so good. The paranoia comes through.
4. **potential spoilers in this comment section for any reader who hasn't seen the movie yet**
5. If u want some more great 70s thrillers watch Pakulas paranoid trilogy and Blow Out from DePalma
6. Popeye Doyle anyone?

## 3. Just watched Groundhog Day and loved it! It felt so cozy, funny, and meaningful. Can anyone suggest more movies with a similar vibe?

**Score:** 65
**Body:** I recently watched Groundhog Day and completely loved its cozy, thoughtful vibe. I’m looking for similar movies that mix humor, meaning, and comfort. What should I watch next?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Go watch What About Bob for some more Bill Murray, it’s wholesome and hilarious.
2. Not a movie but you might enjoy the TV show Northern Exposure.
3. The Family Man starring Nicholas Cage.
4. About Time, You’ve Got Mail, Pride & Prejudice (2005), The Adjustment Bureau, About a Boy, Notting Hill
5. Why is everyone recommending time loops 🤣 that’s not what the prompt is even asking for. 

As Good As it Gets (Jack Nicholson)

Bucket List

Klaus (2019)

The Family Man

Green Book

Mall Secret Apartment

Junior
  - You’re completely right. I meant the cozy vibe, not the time loop….
  - Oooooh The Family Man.  That’s a good one for what op is asking for!
6. Harvey starring Jimmy Stewart has that cozy charm
7. Amelie
8. When Harry Met Sally

## 4. Movies in which humanity destroys itself

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I watched End of Evangelion earlier this year and, as is to be expected, its stuck with me for months. In particular, I loved how the end of the world was the result of human failure, it could have been avoided, but because we are the way we are, it was inevitable. The people with a death drive pushed humanity to destruction and those resisting failed to stop it.

So thats exactly what Im looking for. More movies where the end comes as the result of people's decisions and failures that could have been avoided. The closest things to it I can think of are Don't Look Up and Threads, both of which I highly recommend.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. CSPAN
  - Man Im gullible, I actually googled CSPAN lol

You guessed the reason Im in the mood for these movies.
2. 12 Monkeys (1995)
3. Children of men
4. Don’t look up
5. Planet of the Apes

Silent Running

Soylent Green
6. WALL-E
7. Dr strangelove
8. Cabin in the Woods
9. Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb (1964) - A satirical take on nuclear war and human fallibility.

Fail-Safe(1964) - A thriller about a flawed military system that could trigger a nuclear war.

The Day After(1983) - A made-for-TV movie depicting the aftermath of a nuclear war.


Children of Men (2006) - A dystopian thriller set in a world where humans have lost the ability to procreate.

The Road(2009) - A haunting adaptation of Cormac McCarthy's novel about a father-son journey through a post-apocalyptic world.

Snowpiercer(2013) - A sci-fi epic set on a train where social commentary highlights human failure.


These films often explore themes of human nature, societal collapse, and the consequences of our actions. You can check them out.

## 5. Suggest me your favourite movie or series from any language and any genre

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I recently finished watching the \*Dark\* series.... Ik, a bit late, but I was too young to understand back then. I honestly liked it. Before that, I watched *All Quiet on the Western Front*, which I absolutely loved. My cousin’s been pushing me to watch *Chungking Express* and *Fallen Angels*, but I think I’ll save those for later.  
In the meantime, I’d love to hear *your* favourites and I'll definitely try them out 😊

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Run, Lola, Run”
2. Das Boot. WWII submarine story. Best watched in German with subtitles.
3. Sopranos,  Peaky Blinders,  Mad Men,  Game of Thrones, Breaking Bad, The Jinx, The Night Of
4. Ciudad de deus or apocalypto
5. Cloud Atlas (2012)

Pandorum (2009)

Elysium (2013)
6. Devils Rejects

Ravenous

Event Horizon
7. One war movie i recently saw and can't believe i didn't see sooner is:
Come and See (1985). 


Other good war films:
 Jojo Rabbit (2019).
 Lone Survivor (2013).
 Inglorious Basterds (2009).
 Platoon (1986).
 The Patriot (2000).
 The Pianist (2002).


My favorite movies:
 Requiem for a Dream (2000). 
 Spun (2002).
 The VVitch (2015).
 Oculus (2013).
 As Above, So Below (2014).
 Hereditary (2018).
 Night of the Living Dead (1968).
 Last Man on Earth (1964).
 Pulp Fiction (1994).
 Weapons (2025).
 Together (2025).
 Parasite (2019).
 The Hateful Eight (2015).


 Shows:
 The Haunting of Hillhouse.
 The Twilight Zone (original).
 Breaking Bad.
 The Sopranos.
 The Walking Dead.
 The Handmaid's Tale
8. r/televisionsuggestions is a subreddit if you're looking specifically for TV shows suggestions.

*I am a bot, and this action was performed automatically. Please [contact the moderators of this subreddit](/message/compose/?to=/r/MovieSuggestions) if you have any questions or concerns.*
9. What’s Up Doc, The Iron Claw, and The Fall Guy are some faves
10. Scott Pilgrim vs. the World and literally anything else that Edgar Wright directed

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which moment in a movie or tv show had you in absolute silence ?

**Score:** 3481
**Body:** The final episode of Chernobyl . 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Every lie we tell incurs a debt to the truth. Sooner or later that debt is paid.”

Jared Harris had to carry so much on his back to bring this series to a close, and he goddamn did it. His delivery at the end, especially this line, is such a testament to his skill as an actor.
  - He's good in everything. Watch The Terror (S1) if you haven't already. 
    - The man is a treasure in TV. His film work is excellent too but the man can really tell a long form story, and whenever he's in a TV show I think he brings the entire thing up a tier.
    - The Expanse is wonderful too. I could listen to him give speeches all day.
2. " It's cheaper. "
  - Everything about that series is amazing. And just before Covid too. Feels more relevant every day.
    -  "OF COURSE I KNOW THEY'RE LISTENING!!!" \*bang bang bang smash crash\*

Calmly, "We need a new telephone."
3. Ned Stark actually getting his head cut off
  - I read the book first, and had to re read that paragraph because I assumed I'd missed something...but no
4. Chernobyl all the episodes were insane but will never forget the Firefighter episode. The beginning when you see them and they look like they are getting better but then you remember what Legasov said about radiation poisoning the episode before . Just so sad but amazing episode / show

## 2. The Mt. Rushmore of Sequels - Agree?  Disagree?

**Score:** 855
**Body:** This is my Mt. Rushmore of sequels - would you change any of them?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The only thing I have to complain about is I can’t nitpick the list at all.
  - We all need this realization sometimes.
2. Hard to argue with any of them.  Wrath of Khan might have to be on this list though.  Hard to replace any of them.
3. The Dark Knight
4. Mad Max 2.
5. Hear me out... Rush Hour 2
  - But I can't understand the words coming out of your mouth
6. Toy Story 2 for the sake of including one animated film
7. Evil Dead 2
8. Wrath of Khan.

## 3. Which performer can portray both a villain and a hero with such skill?

**Score:** 811

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Willem Dafoe can also be Willem Dafriend
  - He's even been Willem DaJesus. And nobody fucks with DaJesus. 😛🎳
2. Gary Oldman
  - He can play EVERYOOOOOOOOONE
3. Robin Williams. He was dope in One Hour Photo.
  - And Insomnia
4. James Spader
5. Arnold. T1 and T2.
6. Waltz just oozes charisma
7. Vincent D’Onofrio

## 4. What are your favorite examples of true scene-stealers, actors who appeared for a few minutes and owned the whole movie?

**Score:** 440

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Peter Stormare dropping in as the devil in Constantine
2. Billy Crystal as Miracle Max in The Princess Bride

>The King's stinking son fired me, and thank you so much for bringing up such a painful subject. While you're at it, why don't you give me a nice paper cut and pour lemon juice on it? We're closed.
3. Bronson Pinchot in Beverly Hills Cop
4. Alec Baldwin in Glengarry Glen Ross.
5. Christopher Walken in Pulp Fiction.
  - And True Romance; he’s literally only in one scene in both movies.
6. Harvey Keitel's The Wolf in Pulp Fiction
7. Gene Hackman was known for being such a curmudgeon, but in Young Frankenstein he genuinely seems to be having a wonderful time. I don't know many his movies where he has that vibe. Maybe parts of The Bird Cage or The Royal Tenebaums. Then again, he was a great actor-- so it could have been acting.
8. Tom Cruise as Les Grossman in Tropic Thunder.
  - "First of all, take a step back and literally FUCK YOUR OWN FACE!!!"

"Find out who that was, will you?"

## 5. What movie hit you so hard you still think about it years later?

**Score:** 343

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Green Mile
2. Dark City
3. Bro add the movie name ffs.. why’s it not mandatory in this sub I don’t get it
  - It annoys me too bro. You literally looked up a picture of the movie, could it be that damn hard to type it into the post ffs? Movie is a Parasite btw
  - It’s called Parasite.
    - I kno that . But the sub should mandate movie names .. not everyone would know
      - I fucking hate when people don’t post the title, the most braindead activity. And it happens too often. Imagine watching a scene from YouTube and wanting to know where it’s from. Too bad it’s not in the description or the title. Not even the first 200 comments, just a bunch of bums reminiscing about it. Fucking dumb as shit.
4. You think that’s air your breathing Neo?
  - It’s my fart
5. Requiem for a Dream. I’ve struggled similarly. That movie hits hard

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. While comparatively underdiscussed, Peter Weir is clearly one of the GOATs, and deserves even wider appreciation

**Score:** 62
**Body:** There's a lot of directors who are not that well-known but had long and varied careers, and gave us not a one-hit wonder but a fine amount of S-tier movies. Not merely good, but amazing movies, and plenty of them.
I want to talk about one filmmaker who is not a household name but directed some stone-cold classics: Peter Weir.  It seems to me that even in cinephile circles, he is somewhat underdiscussed.

You are probably familiar with the 1975 masterpiece **Picnic at Hanging Rock** (1975), arguably the best Australian film ever made. For those who are not familiar, it's about what happens when a girls boarding school takes a field trip to an unusual but scenic volcanic formation called Hanging Rock. Several other girls venture off despite the rules forbidding them to do so. Set in the early 1900s, it's mysterious and really eerie, a spellbinding movie that some have considered to be almost horror in essence. 

Now if Weir only made this film, he would've made his name already. But that's not even his most famous movie! 

Weir also directed **Dead Poets Society**, a beloved classic that's absolutely adored among Gen Z too who resonates deeply with it.
There's **Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World**, and **The Truman Show**, and the great Harrison Ford movie **Witness** from 1985. 

Another superb Australian film is **The Last Wave** (1977). A business lawyer David Burton is assigned to defend five Aboriginals accused of a murder. None of them are willing to speak about what happened, even in their own defense, and the medical examiner can't figure out how the victim died. In the process, David learns disturbing things about himself starts to have increasingly terrifying apocalyptic visions.



Quite a few of his movies possess an ambivalence with a slight otherworldliness to it. As Daniel Joyaux says in a great piece on the director:


>All of Weir’s films, in one way or another, are about people who find themselves out of place and somewhere they don’t quite belong—geographically, sociologically, occupationally, ideologically—and the films then play out the consequences of that wrongness for both the protagonists and, perhaps more crucially, those they encounter. 

>Despite the obvious thematic throughline in his work, Weir’s films marvelously adapt in tone and genre to the specifics of the story they’re telling. Every one of them has worked both on its own terms and as part of Weir’s authorship.

>In some ways, the plots of Weir’s films could easily work as fish-out-of-water comedies, and in a few cases, they sort of have. But in most cases, **his films eschew the humor of being out of place and go straight for the jugular of deeply affecting human drama.**


 Let's talk about him more as an auteur of the highest caliber!

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I am a big Peter Weir fan and would absolutely agree that he's underrated and a filmmaker we should be discussing and analyzing more. He has some great films in his resume and a pretty high batting average. He also demonstrated a commendable versatility, a willingness to continually take on new creative challenges. 

At the same time, if we're talking about Greatest of All Time status, that means we're putting him with the Kubricks and Hitchcocks of the world, with the most groundbreaking, influential filmmakers in history. I'm not entirely sure he fits **there**, although I'd be open to hearing that argument.

I'm also not sure why we're talking about whether his movies deserve to be in the Criterion Collection. Criterion is a distributor, not a movie hall of fame, and whether or not a movie gets a Criterion release has as much to do with who owns the rights as with anything else.
  - I think at the *very* least, Picnic at Hanging Rock deserves to be in conversation about "greatest of all time".  I don't think it's #1 but it's definitely floating somewhere in my top 20 or so.
    - For what it's worth, it finished tied for 408th in the most recent BFI/Sight and Sound poll.
2. The thing I love about Weir is how individualized his filmography is - he made huge, sprawling epics and intimate, personal tales of the human condition, but not a single film is alike to another in tone (maybe in theme). He was fantastic at moving between modes and could work in his own country or internationally and create magic. The Blank Check podcast seems to be preparing for a series on him next year, hopefully it'll bring his filmography back into the light. My personal favourites are Picnic and Master & Commander, but there's something amazing in each of his works. The only thing that didn't land for me was his short film Michael, which was part of the Australian triptych showcase Three To Go from 1971, but only because it's sandwiched between two much better shorts.
  - good to know The Blank Check podcast is doing that 

he was varied in his efforts, and successful at them

whereas many other filmmakers might've just had one or two decades of a good run, making their biggest hits then, and stayed in their home country, Weir succeeded into the 90s and 2000s with movies that were even more famous and widely beloved while retaining artistic achievement
3. *Picnic* is absolutely fantastic, one of the true greats, but I'm generally of the opinion that he turns in mostly good work rather than consistently outstanding films. I remember seeing his film *The Way Back* when it came out and not feeling much other than using the gulag to make a clumsy escape/spirit film. Same with the ones you've named (not seen *The Last Wave*) - they're all very watchable but I don't think they go really deep on their ideas, rather they present an artier take on otherwise mainstream ideas.

His films always interesting technically, like the sound on *Master and Commander* \- whoah - but I reckon he's a solid post-50s B-tier Anglophone director: one untouchable great, nothing bad, underrated.
  - A follow-up question.

Who else would you put in that "b-tier?"

And I'd argue (along with a lot of people) that *The Truman Show* gives him two capital-G Great films in his filmography & that that movie does engage with some really interesting, thought-provoking ideas.
    - just in terms of US/UK/Aus makers of post-war narrative cinema I'd go people Norman Jewison, Mike Nichols, Peter Bogdanovich, Albert Brooks, Alexander Payne, John Sayles. Quality directors all!

Truman Show probably his second best, and I'll try and get to The Lost Wave and possibly reassess!

## 2. The legacy of Terry Gilliam

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Thought I'd start a thread on a filmmaker who hasn't gotten much discussion on here.

If you count *Monty Python and the Holy Grail*, which he codirected with fellow Python Terry Jones, Terry Gilliam's filmography includes 13 films as a director, plus a handful of (mostly Python-related) writing credits and a few shorts. He's a filmmaker with some clear auteur traits (frequent use of fisheye lenses, flying as a visual metaphor for freedom) and some true cult classics in his filmography. He's also a filmmaker notorious for out-of-control productions and conflicts with studio executives.

It's 2025. Gilliam is 84 years old and, barring some late career miracle, unlikely to significantly add to his body of work. Looking at that body of work as a whole, what do you think Gilliam's legacy is? Would you call him a major filmmaker, historically speaking? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If he had only made Brazil (1985) and nothing else ever he’d still be one of the best. 

But he has 

Time Bandits
The Adventures of Baron Munchausen 
12 Monkeys
The Fischer King
Fear & Loathing in Los Vegas
The Imaginarium of Doctor Parnusus (you know what no I’m not gonna spell check that) 

He has a *clear* and boy howdy do I mean *cut and dry* influence on everything from South Park to Guillermo Del Toro to the Wachowskis.

He somehow had more piss and vinegar than Ridley Scott. The story of how he [got Brazil](https://youtu.be/stLlpq-l3MA?si=BhFFtMfupFJlz9hr) released is astounding. I think most people would’ve given up or let the ending be ruined. He didn’t budge. He never budged.

You can see a lot of the [banality of evil](https://youtu.be/dLUHlVLyRxA?si=YXwaJBMSQ0zWb5Qq) running through his work… something GDT really ran with in Pan’s Labyrinth, the Shape of Water.. all the way up to that Pinocchio movie. 

“We’re all just ripping off Terry Gilliam.”
-GDT

If you think late career misfires discredit any of that then George Lucas (Red Tails, Phantom Menace), Francis Ford (Megalopolis), Brian DePalma (Bonfire of the Vanities), Billy Friedkin (Deal of the Century) and Peter Bogdanovich (Noises Off) should also be heavily scrutinized. 

But in my book he is a legend. Forever and always.
2. Time Bandits is astounding and gets better with every year. It’s simultaneously one of the funniest and most frightening fantasy films, both dreamlike and realistic. So too is Brazil, but TB edges it for me; there’s something very deep and dark and absurd about that film’s portrayal of childhood experience. I met Terry and Michael Palin at a screening in Edinburgh, wished I’d asked them better questions about it.
3. What is remarkable with Terry Gilliam is that you can just see that it is one of his movies. This is especially the case in his first “proper” movies, Time Bandits, Brazil and Munchhausen. You can clearly see he has some history in animation as his set design is almost an exaggerated version of animation. Of course also knew how to attract magnificent set designers like Norman Garwood. Of course it is just not that as his worlds are always just a bit left of reality if you know what I mean. In his later movie it is not as prevalent anymore, but there’s still some surreal undertones.
4. People talk about Brazil and Time Bandits but in the end I think *12 Monkeys* is actually his most successful film. It's perfectly executed, still holds up beautifully today, I caught a recent screening of it on 35mm and was blown away by how good it is.

I think this is the rare case where studio constraints actually *helped* him: The script was highly commercial and like a puzzle box, tight as a drum, he didn't go off on a million endless flights of fancy that derailed the vision. The cast is similarly grounded - instead of going off and working with weirdo Johnny Depp, he ended up with one of the most grounded actors of his era - Bruce Willis - for his lead. And Bruce acted his ass off - he's so good, and the character is so well drawn. There's so much yearning and longing in his story. The rest of the cast - Madeleine Stowe, Christopher Plummer, Brad Pitt, all bankable grounded actors who were willing to lend themselves to the film in a way where actors like Depp would have enabled Gilliam to go off the deep end. And with the studio holding the budget over his head and expecting a big studio movie, he had no choice but to be restrained in his approach. He worked with an amazing DP, a hell of a composer, and just in general made it all happen in a way that he never really touched again.

*Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas* is, to me, less interesting but at least it was commercially successful - Gilliam basically had a 2-film window where he was delivering good art that also appealed to the masses with big budgets and he was doing them on time and on budget in a way that made him hirable and able to work within the system. After that....yikes.

Gilliam likes to tell a very selfish story about his career where a bunch of capitalist bureaucrats and penny-pinchers held him back and forced him to change his films, but the reality is he became an undisciplined and logistically impossible diva of a director, and instead of trying to look inward and make the films he could make with the budgets he had, carefully editing himself to work within the constraints of his means (like so many other filmmakers before him, from Orson Welles to Martin Scorcese), he spent his time blaming others, attacking great directors like Spielberg as artistically empty because they committed the crime of *being able to work with the studios*, and it's no wonder that his career kind of faded over time. He could never, even when given as much leeway as possible, make a comprehensive and complete film on his own. His only real successes were when he was reined in by tight scripts, grounded collaborators, and ironclad budgeting. Every other filmmaker of his visual caliber - Tim Burton, Jean Pierre Jeunet, Wes Anderson - they could all work within the confines of this system to make their visions real but Terry Gilliam chose to be a petulant brat instead and that's why we haven't seen anything from him in a long time.

I think ultimately he will be remembered as a visionary filmmaker, but one who couldn't work with the people who wanted to work with him. Even when he was given resources and time and patience he pissed it away, and he didn't have the discipline to edit himself or his work to make things that worked in the real world. The film where he had pretty much total creative control - *The Imaginarium of Dr. Parnassus -* is a total mess. Visually interesting, sure, but a mess nonetheless, and it's because in the end he was undisciplined, more interested in being a martyr than in actually making the work, and unable to edit himself.
5. Brazil is a dazzling film. It’s so dense and rich and layered that I don’t expect to ever stop revisiting it. The studio drama behind its release is fascinating, too, but the actual work itself has something important and challenging to say about the way we choose to live our lives in a post industrial society.

Sam’s a character that struggles with fantasizing he’s something he isn’t - like so many real people, he imagines himself to be different and above the petty, bureaucratic world around him. But he isn’t. He’s as much a product of that world as everyone around him is, and that tension is such a rare and difficult subject to approach.
6. I haven’t seen enough of Gilliam’s films to make a significant comment, but I believe he is certainly a major filmmaker, and an auteur director. Even someone who doesn’t know who he is could watch two of his films and say “hm twelve monkeys reminds me of fear and loathing in los vegas” 
His style is definitely more distinctive than most filmmakers. Brazil is not only a cult classic I believe but a classic, with an unforgettable and prescient take on the future of dystopia. Monty python and the holy grail is in consideration for the best comedy of all time. These two films alone have influenced popular culture at least a decent amount.
7. I can’t deny Gilliam’s influence or talent but I find it very difficult to actually watch his films. For some reason his visual style grates on me in all the movies I’ve watched, with the exception of Holy Grail (which suffers from the separate curse of youthful overfamiliarity). I appreciate *why* people love *Brazil*, but I can’t get through it without feeling annoyed. It reminds me of the BBFC’s inability to pass *Texas Chainsaw Massacre* because of its general essence - I can’t pin down a specific complaint and I can’t change things. It’s my loss, no doubt.

## 3. Question about the Golden Age of Hollywood

**Score:** 4
**Body:** In films from this era, married couples are always shown sleeping in separate beds. I've heard that this was something demanded by the Production Code, but if this is true, my question is: to what degree did the people at the Hays Office think/care that this would be accepted by audiences? Was it actually something that couples generally did at the time, or was it just that the Hays people were so puritanical that even the faintest trace of a suggestion of sex was intolerable to them?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. While separate beds and bedrooms were generally more common then, it was completely about censorship. There's a fantastic book on the subject by Louis Chunovic called "One Foot on the Floor: The Curious Evolution of Sex on Television from I Love Lucy to South Park," I read it for a paper in college.
2. I saw this discussed recently, possibly in Mark Cousins’ *The Story of Film* - it was related to the ‘58 movie *Indiscreet*, which used a split screen to make Cary Grant and Ingrid Bergman appear to be in bed together, though it was established beforehand they were alone in bed in different locations. I think it was the director Stanley Donen being interviewed - certainly someone involved in the production - and he said it was very much a tactic to bypass the censors.
3. I don't know if this is a historically correct answer, but I remember reading once that the double bed thing was more of a status symbol. If you shared a bed, it meant you were poor. Wealthy couples had separate bedrooms, and middle class couples would have separate beds. So the depiction in movies was somewhat true to life and not just the production code, as people in Golden Age movies were generally middle to upper class. I noticed this after watching *The Earrings of Madame de...,* which shows a wealthy couple with separate bedrooms (it's also a European film so it's not bound by the production code). 

I also think there was a Hays Code rule that a couple could be in the same bed, but one of them had to have at least one foot on the floor, but maybe I'm making that one up.
4. >Was it actually something that couples generally did at the time

I don't think it was something done at the time. You have to realize, back then religious groups and political/civic organizations had a far stronger grip on culture. The Hays Code was a response to that political pressure from those groups. It's not like Hollywood decided to impose those rules all by its lonesome. It was either create a code and impose it, or have outside groups and politicians rake you through the coals. 

>to what degree did the people at the Hays Office think/care that this would be accepted by audiences?

The audience for movies did not decrease after the code was implemented. And why would it. What alternatives were there? It wasn't until television was in full swing that cracks in the censorship code started to appear.

## 4. Thoughts on Reds (1981)?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** What are your thoughts on Warren Beatty’s Reds?

Reds is a 198 epic that starred Warren Beatty, Diane Keaton, Jack Nicholson, & Maureen Stapleton and it’s about the life of John Reed, who along with his girlfriend, Louise Bryant, chronicles the October Revolution.

I was thinking about the film recently, especially with Dianne Keaton’s passing. I must say, Reds is an interesting film to say the least. I do think, technically, it is Warren Beatty’s best film and possibly his magnum opus. I think it is a complex film with three dimensional characters who all have goals that involved with communism and trying to spread it across the country and over the course of the film, John Reed, who was an idealistic and devoted to the cause, becomes disillusioned to it, especially when he goes to Soviet Russia and dies of typhus with Louise Bryant at his side.

I feel what made the film work was the sheer epicness to it and Vittorio Storraro does a masterful job with the Cinematography. I also found it interesting with Beatty filming what are “The Witnesses”, who all were there during that time and gave perspective on the lives on John Reed and Louise Bryant. I do admired the fact that Warren Beatty did a good job with a complex subject and made a smart film that did not treat the audience like idiots. And everyone was on their A Game with this film, Dianne Keaton and Jack Nicholson giving great performances as Louise Bryant and Eugene O Neal and a whole lot of characters actors that steal the show, with Edward Hermann, Jerzy Koskinski, Maureen Stapleton, and Gene Hackman in a small role as highlights.

While I think Warren Beatty did a great job directing, I think he did a good performance as John Reed, but I feel he was the weakest of the main leads. I also hear from stories that Beatty was very much of a perfectionist during the making of the film, demanding up to 50-85 takes for a scene, which aggravated everyone on the set. But I still commend him on his effort.

Overall, I think Reds is a great epic and I feel the film is underrated as not a lot of people talk about it now, but I think its a good film and is possibly Beatty’s Magnum Opus.



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

Beatty made communists sexy in a way that many communist governments have failed to do again and again, just an all-timer movie.

EDIT: Also just remembered there are like three separate scenes where Beatty dresses down Emma Goldman for having the moral mind of a child, fuck what a great flick.
2. Just blind bought Reds a couple weeks ago on Blu Ray and think it's really good! It is pretty long and honestly a little boring at parts (mostly the first half) but it does a great job of showing history in a nuanced way while also having a really great dynamic between Beatty and Keaton's characters that asks "what do you pick between time with the ones you love or working to build up a new, more equitable world with your own two hands." Either option comes with its own merits and pitfalls and I just found this film to be really mature. The scenes as the Soviet Union is just blossoming are so inspiring in their depiction of community action to me as well!

## 5. An Autumn Afternoon

**Score:** 1
**Body:** In its final act, Yasujiro Ozu's *An Autumn Afternoon* does something pretty unusual: it jumps forward in time from Michiko's match being settled to her actually getting married. Why does it do this? Why don't Ozu and Kogo Noda (his co-screenwriter) show anything between these two moments, given that it arguably matters to how we're supposed to read Michiko's character? David Bordwell mentions this fact in his Criterion commentary, but he doesn't give much of a rationale for the filmmaker's decision (as far as I'm aware; perhaps I'm just inattentive).

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Ellipsis (narrative device) - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellipsis_(narrative_device))

  
Ozu was a big fan of narrative ellipsis and used it quite often throughout his later movies. It usually highlights the context and decision-making around a big event (like a wedding) as more important than the event itself.
2. Bordwell's book on Ozu is available as a free download on his website and it goes into a lot of detail on what he calls Ozu's playful ways of subverting audience expectations, eg, setting us up for scenes that either aren't shown or were planned but never transpired
